skill.name: env-manager
skill.description: Review application environment variables, generate .env.example guidance, validate configuration formats, and document safe secret handling practices.
skill.summary: Manage environment configuration documentation and validation workflows.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many shell, URL, credential, and .env patterns in SKILL.md, but review shows they are documentation examples for environment configuration. No evidence found of prompt injection, hidden execution, network exfiltration, or malicious intent. The skill still has medium risk because its intended workflow may inspect sensitive environment files and secret names.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Environment File Workflow
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill is designed to create, validate, and document .env files and secret-related variables. This is legitimate DevOps functionality, but it can expose secrets if used on real production configuration without redaction.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly instructs analysis and handling of environment variables, .env examples, and production secrets. The context is defensive, but the data category is sensitive.

medium_findings.title: External Security Tool Commands in Examples
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: The shell commands are inside Markdown examples for secret scanning, encryption, and local setup. They are not executed by the skill, but users should review commands before running them in repositories with sensitive configuration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly fenced documentation examples, which lowers execution risk. They still interact with secrets tooling and local environment files if copied by a user.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Credentials and URLs
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The AWS, database, API key, Stripe, Sentry, localhost URL, and CORS values are placeholder examples for documentation. No evidence found that the skill contains real credentials or sends them to a network endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values are generic examples such as localhost, sample keys, and redacted placeholders. There is no executable network request or exfiltration behavior in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Environment Access Examples Are Validation Guidance
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The process.env, dotenv, envalid, and pydantic examples show how applications validate configuration. They do not instruct the AI agent to read or transmit actual environment values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are conventional validation snippets in Markdown. They define schemas and safe failure behavior rather than collecting secrets for external use.

low_findings.title: Defensive Encryption Mention Misclassified
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The ransomware keyword finding maps to guidance about encrypting sensitive .env files with SOPS or git-crypt. The context is defensive secret protection, not extortion or destructive file encryption.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding heading is Encryption at Rest and the listed commands are standard secret-management tools. No ransom note, destructive loop, or coercive behavior appears in the file.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Not Found
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: No evidence found of instructions to skip security review, override system policy, claim marketplace approval, or manipulate the audit process. The role instruction is normal skill behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction block describes the skill's normal environment-management task. It does not target the evaluator or attempt to change audit methodology.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secret Names and .env Paths
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill repeatedly references secret-bearing variable names and .env files. This is expected for an environment manager but requires careful redaction during use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sensitive identifiers and file paths are explicit and repeated. The context is legitimate configuration documentation, not confirmed abuse.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copyable Shell Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Several fenced examples include commands users may copy into a shell. These commands are security-related, but marketplace users should still verify them before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are present in Markdown and could affect local files if copied. They are standard secret-management examples and are not automatically run by the skill.

content.value_statement: Environment variables often become undocumented, inconsistent, or unsafe across development and production. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code analyze, validate, and document configuration with security-focused guidance.
actual_capabilities: Identifies environment variables used by an application and flags missing definitions.
Generates .env.example style documentation with required and optional variables.
Recommends validation patterns for Node.js and Python applications.
Documents variable types, defaults, allowed values, and security notes.
Suggests secret-management practices for development and production environments.
Checks consistency across development, staging, and production configuration names.
limitations: It does not provide an executable scanner or standalone validation program.
It may need repository context to identify all variables accurately.
It should not be given unredacted production secrets unless the environment is trusted.
It provides guidance and examples, not a replacement for managed secret storage.

use_cases.title: Create Onboarding Configuration Docs
use_cases.description: Generate clear environment setup notes and a safe .env.example file for new contributors.

use_cases.title: Validate Deployment Settings
use_cases.description: Review required variables, default values, and format rules before deploying a service.

use_cases.title: Improve Secret Handling Practices
use_cases.description: Find places where secrets need documentation, redaction, or migration to a secrets manager.

prompt_templates.title: List Environment Variables
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this project and list the environment variables it appears to use. Separate required variables from optional variables.

prompt_templates.title: Generate Safe Setup Documentation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create environment setup documentation for this project. Include placeholder values only, and do not include any real secrets.

prompt_templates.title: Validate Configuration Rules
prompt_templates.prompt: Suggest validation rules for these environment variables. Include expected type, allowed values, defaults, and clear error messages.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Multi-Environment Consistency
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare development, staging, and production configuration requirements. Identify naming drift, missing variables, unsafe defaults, and secret-management gaps.

output_examples.input: Review a service that uses database, Redis, JWT, AWS, and email provider variables.
output: Required variables are grouped by service area.
Missing documentation is flagged for JWT and email provider secrets.
Recommended placeholders are used instead of real credential values.

output_examples.input: Create deployment configuration guidance for staging and production.
output: The response separates shared settings from environment-specific settings.
Production-only variables are marked as secret-managed.
Unsafe debug and SSL settings are called out for review.

best_practices: Use placeholder values in examples and redact all production secrets before sharing context.
Validate environment variables during application startup with explicit error messages.
Store production secrets in a managed secret store instead of committed files.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real API keys, database passwords, or private keys into prompts.
Do not commit .env files that contain live credentials.
Do not rely on silent defaults for required production configuration.

faq.question: Can this skill generate a .env.example file?
faq.answer: Yes. It can draft a .env.example style template with placeholder values and documentation.

faq.question: Does this skill execute validation commands?
faq.answer: No. The skill provides guidance and examples. Users decide which commands or validation code to run.

faq.question: Can I use it with production secrets?
faq.answer: Use redacted values whenever possible. Avoid sharing live secrets unless the execution environment is trusted.

faq.question: Which languages does it cover?
faq.answer: The examples focus on Node.js and Python, but the documentation approach is broadly applicable.

faq.question: Can it find hardcoded configuration values?
faq.answer: Yes. With repository context, it can point out values that should likely become environment variables.

faq.question: Is it safe for public repositories?
faq.answer: It is useful for public repositories when outputs use placeholders and avoid exposing real credentials.
